speaker: interviewer
text: We have an interview with Alberta Szalita. Today is December 11th, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: What we've been through? And where did we stop? We are in Warsaw. I am this medical student. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I could mention here that, at that time, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anti-Semitism was already much more visible, around. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I personally did not feel affected by it, to speak about it much. But you felt it already in, in medical circles. And you read about it in the papers, or you heard about it. And they started sometimes to break the windows in Jewish stores. It-- the situation-- the economic situation in Poland was not a good one. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was a lot of people without work--

speaker: interviewer
text: How come it--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that I remember. Particularly [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I-- as I'm-- as I'm going-- I'm coming on-- I'm going on, that my future husband-- I'm just thinking, when did I meet him? I used to go home-- I used to go home. I mean, my mother lived at that time in Luck with her-- with my grandparents, with her parents. I liked very much my grandmother. And she, in those years, was a quiet woman that didn't spoke-- didn't speak much. And she used to like to sit with us girls, particularly, to listen what we are talking about, and what we are telling to each other. I used to wait-- I missed my, my home. I used to wait, wait to get away to, to go home for vacation. And I used to collect [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to collect things to bring my little sister, whom I loved very much. And I should have mentioned her. She was born in Wlodzimierz-- in Volodymyr-Volynsky. In Polish, it was Wlodzimierz-- where I finish gymnasium. And, and as I mentioned, my mother moved to Luck, where her parents lived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was 15 years younger than myself, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Franka. And she came one day to, to visit, visit me in Warsaw. A friend of, of, of ours, Mark Mark Rubenstein, was-- brought her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Again, it's a mixed situation, because my sister, Mina, was working for my-- the cousin-- one of the cousins there, with a daughter Franka's age. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, that would mean to introduce you to that, that aunt of mine, which I already talked about in Warsaw, who had three daughters and four sons. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] But I'll, I'll leave this for the time being because we'll have time to talk about it at some other opportunity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I used to come to, to Luck for the provocation. And I met medical-- all kinds of students who, who came also at vacation time. [SIGHS] And when I-- it was after the second year of medicine, I went-- in summertime, I went to the local Jewish hospital in Luck, where the director was my future father-in-law, Dr. Pemow, Friedman Pemow, that you heard about. And his son was only a student at that time in, in near Danzig in Germany. He studied to be an engineer. It was the only son they had, Zenobi, or, for short, [? Zonya ?]. And there was a, a, a flood. And he came to take pictures of that flood in the hospital. This is how we met. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we used to go, all of us, used to go to the beach. There is a Styr-- a river, Styr, in Luck. We used to go to, to swim, to, to, to spend some time on the beach. And he will-- [? Zonya ?] used to also come to that beach. So I met him. And I met a number of-- plenty of students. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then he went to Warsaw, that [? Zonya ?]. He was looking for a job-- couldn't find a job. He'd been graduated, graduated as an engineer, couldn't find a job. And we met and started to go out together with him and, and [? Marek ?]. That's the one that brought my Franka, my little sister, to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to spend a lot of time together. Whenever there was time, we used to go to concerts. We used to go in the evenings sometimes to, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to theater, so whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Around-- since you are interested in-- particularly in the issues of Jewishness, there was more and more felt that anti-Semitism was growing. And in addition to that, as time went on, some people from Germany came to Poland because of what was going on in Germany. So then of course, one heard enough about--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking about the time-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm still-- if I'm still a student, it means before '35.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So that's, that's the time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We got married in '37-- with him. But he was in Warsaw for a couple of years. And he was-- he couldn't find a job in Warsaw. And finally we found a job in Katowice, in Slask. That-- it's Silesia, if you know where that is. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I graduated-- I graduated in 1936. I was supposed to graduate in 1935. But when I was taking, taking the, the examination, a cousin of mine from this Brzesc-- that's why I was mentioning this. Just that one of the cousins there got married to a Polish-- to a Polish Jewish girl from, from Warsaw. And she came to be delivered of her child and got infected in Warsaw. And they were thinking about-- that she was going to die. And anyway, as I was sitting and studying for an exam, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her husband found me where I was studying to come to see what we can do about his wife. Well, I was busy with her for a good, good six weeks, or maybe two months.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I, I had to take my examinations in the Fall. And therefore, I graduated June-- I graduated in January. That was in June what happened with her. And I-- or maybe it was in May. But I cannot tell  you exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in January-- I graduated on January 12th, 1936. It means half a-- half a year later than my class. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I started specialization. I was planning to be a neurologist for children. So I was-- I was accepted in any place that I wanted to, to do my specialization. And I did it in university clinics-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] neurology. Pediatrics-- I was at the same time going to pediatric hospital, which was a Jewish hospital, [NON-ENGLISH]. It was called in the name of Bergson and [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was close to the university clinic of neurology. So I could combine-- spend time in one place and the other. And the reason I'm mentioning it is because in that particular Jewish hospital for children, I got a job when the war broke out. So that's why I'm talking about it. But at any rate, I did spend the three years of specialization between the, the university clinic, that was the [NON-ENGLISH], as it was called, and the university hospital, and, and that children's hospital. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] I continued-- I continued-- it started-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a-- I met in Luck in summertime a girl by the name Gina Zabludowska, with whom we became very close friends. They lived in Warsaw. And during my Warsaw years, at the times we did El Dorado and all that, we were very, very friendly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that [? Zonya ?] knew the youngest sister of that Gina. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I'm just thinking, well, that's a transition, so to speak, to the future. Because Gina left in 1937. She went-- left to Israel. She is in Israel. She's a psychologist. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Her sister also left to-- left and went to Israel. And her family-- her father and mother, perished under the Germans when-- subsequently, when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made them go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: What made them go to Israel? It's the same thing. I mean, it's the-- they didn't see any future for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: No future.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? I don't-- I don't think that they were particularly engaged in any kind of Zionist organization, or whatever. But it was at that time, youth, Jewish youth, did not see any future for oneself. And then [? Zonya ?] couldn't find a job-- the same thing. I mean, when I would-- we got-- we became close. And we were planning to get married. And we were even-- actually wanting even to go away somewhere to Australia, or wherever. Because it didn't look like there would be any possibility to settle, to find a job, to find work. That was-- it didn't look like you can build on something. And at the same time, oh yeah, [? Zonya ?], who, who studied in Germany, he knew what to-- he, he knew more about the anti-Semitism in Germany than I ever would know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think I'm, I'm going to skip now the rest of it. We will just start with the war. Because there's a lot still to, to be talking about in terms of changes. So it came 1939. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the situation got more tense in, in Warsaw [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because of what was going on in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And you couldn't get a visa to go away someplace. I remember I was interested in it. I was interested more than [? Zonya ?] because he was an only son. And he could not-- did not feel so simple about-- to, to leave his parents. His father, as I mentioned, during the year-- I know what I was struggling with. I was going to talk for a few minutes about the role of his father during my life while I was still a student. Because after the second year, we have-- we start clinical work in-- in-- in medicine. The first two years is what is, in this country, pre-med-- chemistry, anatomy, histology-- all this preparatory kind of things for the clinical work. And he liked to have a student in me. So he was really giving me books to read, cases to discuss. He was a very good teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: So before I started the clinic-- clinical work in-- in Warsaw, I was already beginning to be familiar with clinical work in, in the hospital. The hospital was a general hospital. That I went to the general hospital the first day, so I thought I'm not going to be able to continue medicine.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first-- we started the, the obstetrics. And the women were mourning and groaning preparing to get them to get to, to have a child, two women particularly. Then they took me around the hospital, making the rounds. And one man was-- had jaundice and looked terrible. And I started to feel like I'm going to faint. I got pale. I walked out to the hall of the hospital. And I needed to wash my face with something cold. I really almost fainted.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came home from the hospital, I said I couldn't eat. And I, I lie down. And I said, I cannot go to the hospital next day. I mean, I'm, I'm no good to be-- to be a physician, or whatever. I felt very bad physically. But then I was meditating. What I am I going to do? And then the next day, I went back to the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, yeah, well, well, but the same day was happening a case that particularly made a terrible impression on me. There was a Jew that ate chicken on Friday evening and swallowed a bone. And the bone went down to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to his-- down-- and his genitals became swollen. The testes were, were swollen like, like a-- like a small pail. And all that, I saw in the one day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So naturally, it gave me a shock. But I went next day to the hospital and continued. And I learned quite a lot from, from him. And we knew each other. We had a very good relationship with each other. But of course, at that time, neither did I know, nor did, did he know that we-- exactly his son and myself are going to, to be married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say you were a specially exceptional student in the medical school?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. That's one--

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, would say that I was a-- sort of an exceptional student any place I studied.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. You were, like, an honor student?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not necessarily getting honors. I was not for, for the grades. But I was-- I was a good student. People saw something exceptional in me. It doesn't mean I felt anything exceptional. But I just was a-- I had a very good memory, to begin with. I still, even today, can say that I'm still having some good memory, even though it's not the same memory as I-- as it was even 10 years ago, five years ago, whatever. But I was a good student. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I learned a lot from him because he wanted-- he wanted to teach me. He was a generous teacher. And I was eager to, to learn. I decided that I wanted to be a physician at the age of 15, something like this. The reasons-- the reason-- I know whether that's the reason. But I-- when I think now about it, is that I think it's my mother affected me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At the age of-- at that time, what happened is that a neighbor in Wlodzimierz, in Volodymyr-Volynsky, when I was in gymnasium, and when my parents were still there, a neighbor of ours got intoxicated with carbon monoxide. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were awakened up in the middle of the night because she was unconscious. And I, I got up also and learned what was happening with her. So I wanted-- that, that year, particularly in this fifth class of gymnasium, there we had it in our-- in the book, first aid. So I read what is the first aid, what to do under such circumstances. And I ran into that house. And all I had to do is to open a window and put her head in hot water. That's what they gave as a-- as a remedy. And sure enough, she opened her eyes. And so the next day, it was like I saved her life. And I'm not-- I cannot say that I really saved her life, or whatever. But, but that at any rate, that was necessary to do. And I read at that time a book-- a book which was written by [INAUDIBLE]. And it was called, which means 'homeless people.' And the book is about a doctor [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who worked [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in, in, in poverty circles-- in, in the book. And he was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] serving people. And that was-- appealed to me. So whether it's a book, or whether it's the admiration, that I, all of a sudden, in the-- in the eyes of that neighbor, I was somebody, like, saving her. Or whether it was, because whenever somebody was sick in that shtetl, they used to call my mother to, to take care of them, to help take care-- and she liked to do it. This I'm adding now, after years, that maybe that was my mother's contribution to it also. But that's what I decided that I want to be-- I want to do. And, of course, in those years, you know, one did not so easily use women as doctors to begin with. Like, for example, in Warsaw, women-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a woman was not a general practitioner. A woman was not a surgeon. In, in Warsaw, there were two women surgeons. One was the wife of the Professor [INAUDIBLE]. And the other was my partner that-- that I mentioned. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [? Mira ?] was her name, from Lodz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well-- Maybe we can go forward to the time that you mentioned that you and your future husband were thinking of leaving, because things got worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was thinking that we should go away somewhere because he could not find a job. And I was approaching a specialization. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in my field, the field that I-- that I selected, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was a combination of pediatrics with neurology, I probably felt that I would-- that I would get enough work. And besides, the minute I graduated, I was talking about my graduation, that Zabludowska-- Gina, that was my friend-- her father had high blood pressure. And the day I got my degree, he wanted me to be his doctor. That's probably why I was going to talk about them. Because I became his doctor right after I just got officially [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] graduated. And I-- he wanted me to be his-- people had confidence in me. Come to say it in a brief sentence, for some reason, people had confidence in me. The same thing happened with this-- with-- where my-- if I gave an ad in the paper that I'm giving lessons. Anytime that I went to see the family, they, they hired me. It wasn't the case that, that somebody would not-- would not accept me as that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what-- the tutor. Actually, it's tutorship that I did at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So people did have confidence in me. That-- that I felt. That-- that I knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, so we were not-- we, we even tried something, I think to-- because I had talked to him into it to see whether we could go to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Australia. But you couldn't get a visa. That's what-- that's what he found out. Well, anyway, so-- there we, we got married. And we lived in, in, in Warsaw. And he, he was receiving money from his father, didn't have a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: '37. November 3rd, '37. That, I remember the date. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then a friend of his helped him get a job in, in Katowice. Katowice, that's, that's near the Austrian-- it was near Czechoslovakia. Silesia-- that's what I mentioned before.

speaker: interviewee
text: So exactly at that time, the director of this children's hospital, when the war broke out, offered me a job-- a job meaning a paying job. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, for the same reason, it felt like to-- for war time, I should-- I should be there working in that hospital. So that's-- I say the same thing, that-- why he had confidence in me. Offered me the job, and I accepted it. And I was thinking that he's going to go to the army because that age group was to go to, to service to, to the army. Besides, I did not want to feel responsible for this only son. And he didn't want to leave without me. So I had to resign from that job. In the meantime, the job consisted of that we thought that the Germans are going to use-- to use gas. So we were making masks for the children in case there would be-- because they started to bomb Warsaw right away. That was-- How did you experience--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: The war started--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So there--

speaker: interviewer
text: --breaking out?

speaker: interviewee
text: --September 1st, Friday, the Germans attacked Poland. So they were bombing the city the same day. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was going to work to the hospital. And, you know, well, it-- well, initially, the first day, one didn't know yet what-- how-- what kind of a form it will take. On Sunday, the 3rd of September, the French and the English also-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, they did declare war with the Germans. And I went-- that was one time I went to some demonstration to the French embassy and the German-- the French and the English embassies, to demonstrate support. Just to, like, to be-- so grateful we were that they also decided to take part in that war. And, of course, one thought that they will do something. In the meantime, however, there was more bombing. And in the hospital, we were busy preparing for gas from-- for the children. Well, I had to tell this [INAUDIBLE]. And she used to use me also when she-- this-- the director of the hospital used to send me to some patients sometimes when they called for a doctor, pediatrician. So she used to-- so-- to patronize me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK.
